<p><strong>Source:</strong> <a href="https://github.com/QubesOS/qubes-secpack/blob/master/QSBs/qsb-088-2023.txt.sig.simon">https://github.com/QubesOS/qubes-secpack/blob/master/QSBs/qsb-088-2023.txt.sig.simon</a></p>

<h2 id="what-is-the-purpose-of-this-announcement">What is the purpose of this announcement?</h2>

<p>The purpose of this announcement is to inform the Qubes community that a new Qubes Security Bulletin (QSB) has been published.</p>

<h2 id="what-is-a-qubes-security-bulletin-qsb">What is a Qubes Security Bulletin (QSB)?</h2>

<p>A Qubes security bulletin (QSB) is a security announcement issued by the <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/security/#qubes-security-team">Qubes security team</a>. A QSB typically provides a summary and impact analysis of one or more recently-discovered software vulnerabilities, including details about patching to address them. A list of all QSBs is available <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/security/qsb/">here</a>.</p>

<h2 id="why-should-i-care-about-qsbs">Why should I care about QSBs?</h2>

<p>QSBs tell you what actions you must take in order to protect yourself from recently-discovered security vulnerabilities. In most cases, security vulnerabilities are addressed by <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/doc/how-to-update/">updating normally</a>. However, in some cases, special user action is required. In all cases, the required actions are detailed in QSBs.</p>

<h2 id="what-are-the-pgp-signatures-that-accompany-qsbs">What are the PGP signatures that accompany QSBs?</h2>

<p>A <a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pretty_Good_Privacy">PGP</a> signature is a cryptographic <a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Digital_signature">digital signature</a> made in accordance with the <a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pretty_Good_Privacy#OpenPGP">OpenPGP</a> standard. PGP signatures can be cryptographically verified with programs like <a href="https://gnupg.org/">GNU Privacy Guard (GPG)</a>. The Qubes security team cryptographically signs all QSBs so that Qubes users have a reliable way to check whether QSBs are genuine. The only way to be certain that a QSB is authentic is by verifying its PGP signatures.</p>

<h2 id="why-should-i-care-whether-a-qsb-is-authentic">Why should I care whether a QSB is authentic?</h2>

<p>A forged QSB could deceive you into taking actions that adversely affect the security of your Qubes OS system, such as installing malware or making configuration changes that render your system vulnerable to attack. Falsified QSBs could sow fear, uncertainty, and doubt about the security of Qubes OS or the status of the Qubes OS Project.</p>

<h2 id="how-do-i-verify-the-pgp-signatures-on-a-qsb">How do I verify the PGP signatures on a QSB?</h2>

<p>The following command-line instructions assume a Linux system with <code class="language-plaintext highlighter-rouge">git</code> and <code class="language-plaintext highlighter-rouge">gpg</code> installed. (See <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/security/verifying-signatures/#openpgp-software">here</a> for Windows and Mac options.)</p>

<ol>
  <li>
    <p>Obtain the Qubes Master Signing Key (QMSK), e.g.:</p>

    <div class="language-shell_session highlighter-rouge"><div class="highlight"><pre class="highlight"><code><span class="gp">$</span><span class="w"> </span>gpg <span class="nt">--fetch-keys</span> https://keys.qubes-os.org/keys/qubes-master-signing-key.asc
<span class="go">gpg: directory '/home/user/.gnupg' created
gpg: keybox '/home/user/.gnupg/pubring.kbx' created
gpg: requesting key from 'https://keys.qubes-os.org/keys/qubes-master-signing-key.asc'
gpg: /home/user/.gnupg/trustdb.gpg: trustdb created
gpg: key DDFA1A3E36879494: public key "Qubes Master Signing Key" imported
gpg: Total number processed: 1
gpg:               imported: 1
</span></code></pre></div>    </div>

    <p>(See <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/security/verifying-signatures/#how-to-import-and-authenticate-the-qubes-master-signing-key">here</a> for more ways to obtain the QMSK.)</p>
  </li>
  <li>
    <p>View the fingerprint of the PGP key you just imported. (Note: <code class="language-plaintext highlighter-rouge">gpg&gt;</code> indicates a prompt inside of the GnuPG program. Type what appears after it when prompted.)</p>

    <div class="language-shell_session highlighter-rouge"><div class="highlight"><pre class="highlight"><code><span class="gp">$</span><span class="w"> </span>gpg <span class="nt">--edit-key</span> 0x427F11FD0FAA4B080123F01CDDFA1A3E36879494
<span class="gp">gpg (GnuPG) 2.2.27;</span><span class="w"> </span>Copyright <span class="o">(</span>C<span class="o">)</span> 2021 Free Software Foundation, Inc.
<span class="go">This is free software: you are free to change and redistribute it.
There is NO WARRANTY, to the extent permitted by law.
   
   
pub  rsa4096/DDFA1A3E36879494
     created: 2010-04-01  expires: never       usage: SC
     trust: unknown       validity: unknown
[ unknown] (1). Qubes Master Signing Key
   
</span><span class="gp">gpg&gt;</span><span class="w"> </span>fpr
<span class="go">pub   rsa4096/DDFA1A3E36879494 2010-04-01 Qubes Master Signing Key
 Primary key fingerprint: 427F 11FD 0FAA 4B08 0123  F01C DDFA 1A3E 3687 9494
</span></code></pre></div>    </div>
  </li>
  <li>
    <p><strong>Important:</strong> At this point, you still don’t know whether the key you just imported is the genuine QMSK or a forgery. In order for this entire procedure to provide meaningful security benefits, you <em>must</em> authenticate the QMSK out-of-band. <strong>Do not skip this step!</strong> The standard method is to obtain the QMSK fingerprint from <em>multiple independent sources in several different ways</em> and check to see whether they match the key you just imported. See <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/security/verifying-signatures/#how-to-import-and-authenticate-the-qubes-master-signing-key">here</a> for more details and ideas for how to do that.</p>

    <p><strong>Tip:</strong> Record the genuine QMSK fingerprint in a safe place (or several) so that you don’t have to repeat this step in the future.</p>
  </li>
  <li>
    <p>Once you are satisfied that you have the genuine QMSK, set its trust level to 5 (“ultimate”), then quit GnuPG with <code class="language-plaintext highlighter-rouge">q</code>.</p>

    <div class="language-shell_session highlighter-rouge"><div class="highlight"><pre class="highlight"><code><span class="gp">gpg&gt;</span><span class="w"> </span>trust
<span class="go">pub  rsa4096/DDFA1A3E36879494
     created: 2010-04-01  expires: never       usage: SC
     trust: unknown       validity: unknown
[ unknown] (1). Qubes Master Signing Key
   
Please decide how far you trust this user to correctly verify other users' keys
(by looking at passports, checking fingerprints from different sources, etc.)
   
  1 = I don't know or won't say
  2 = I do NOT trust
  3 = I trust marginally
  4 = I trust fully
  5 = I trust ultimately
  m = back to the main menu
   
Your decision? 5
Do you really want to set this key to ultimate trust? (y/N) y
   
pub  rsa4096/DDFA1A3E36879494
     created: 2010-04-01  expires: never       usage: SC
     trust: ultimate      validity: unknown
[ unknown] (1). Qubes Master Signing Key
Please note that the shown key validity is not necessarily correct
unless you restart the program.
   
</span><span class="gp">gpg&gt;</span><span class="w"> </span>q
</code></pre></div>    </div>
  </li>
  <li>
    <p>Use Git to clone the qubes-secpack repo.</p>

    <div class="language-shell_session highlighter-rouge"><div class="highlight"><pre class="highlight"><code><span class="gp">$</span><span class="w"> </span>git clone https://github.com/QubesOS/qubes-secpack.git
<span class="go">Cloning into 'qubes-secpack'...
remote: Enumerating objects: 4065, done.
remote: Counting objects: 100% (1474/1474), done.
remote: Compressing objects: 100% (742/742), done.
remote: Total 4065 (delta 743), reused 1413 (delta 731), pack-reused 2591
Receiving objects: 100% (4065/4065), 1.64 MiB | 2.53 MiB/s, done.
Resolving deltas: 100% (1910/1910), done.
</span></code></pre></div>    </div>
  </li>
  <li>
    <p>Import the included PGP keys. (See our <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/security/pack/#pgp-key-policies">PGP key policies</a> for important information about these keys.)</p>

    <div class="language-shell_session highlighter-rouge"><div class="highlight"><pre class="highlight"><code><span class="gp">$</span><span class="w"> </span>gpg <span class="nt">--import</span> qubes-secpack/keys/<span class="k">*</span>/<span class="k">*</span>
<span class="go">gpg: key 063938BA42CFA724: public key "Marek Marczykowski-Górecki (Qubes OS signing key)" imported
gpg: qubes-secpack/keys/core-devs/retired: read error: Is a directory
gpg: no valid OpenPGP data found.
gpg: key 8C05216CE09C093C: 1 signature not checked due to a missing key
gpg: key 8C05216CE09C093C: public key "HW42 (Qubes Signing Key)" imported
gpg: key DA0434BC706E1FCF: public key "Simon Gaiser (Qubes OS signing key)" imported
gpg: key 8CE137352A019A17: 2 signatures not checked due to missing keys
gpg: key 8CE137352A019A17: public key "Andrew David Wong (Qubes Documentation Signing Key)" imported
gpg: key AAA743B42FBC07A9: public key "Brennan Novak (Qubes Website &amp; Documentation Signing)" imported
gpg: key B6A0BB95CA74A5C3: public key "Joanna Rutkowska (Qubes Documentation Signing Key)" imported
gpg: key F32894BE9684938A: public key "Marek Marczykowski-Górecki (Qubes Documentation Signing Key)" imported
gpg: key 6E7A27B909DAFB92: public key "Hakisho Nukama (Qubes Documentation Signing Key)" imported
gpg: key 485C7504F27D0A72: 1 signature not checked due to a missing key
gpg: key 485C7504F27D0A72: public key "Sven Semmler (Qubes Documentation Signing Key)" imported
gpg: key BB52274595B71262: public key "unman (Qubes Documentation Signing Key)" imported
gpg: key DC2F3678D272F2A8: 1 signature not checked due to a missing key
gpg: key DC2F3678D272F2A8: public key "Wojtek Porczyk (Qubes OS documentation signing key)" imported
gpg: key FD64F4F9E9720C4D: 1 signature not checked due to a missing key
gpg: key FD64F4F9E9720C4D: public key "Zrubi (Qubes Documentation Signing Key)" imported
gpg: key DDFA1A3E36879494: "Qubes Master Signing Key" not changed
gpg: key 1848792F9E2795E9: public key "Qubes OS Release 4 Signing Key" imported
gpg: qubes-secpack/keys/release-keys/retired: read error: Is a directory
gpg: no valid OpenPGP data found.
gpg: key D655A4F21830E06A: public key "Marek Marczykowski-Górecki (Qubes security pack)" imported
gpg: key ACC2602F3F48CB21: public key "Qubes OS Security Team" imported
gpg: qubes-secpack/keys/security-team/retired: read error: Is a directory
gpg: no valid OpenPGP data found.
gpg: key 4AC18DE1112E1490: public key "Simon Gaiser (Qubes Security Pack signing key)" imported
gpg: Total number processed: 17
gpg:               imported: 16
gpg:              unchanged: 1
gpg: marginals needed: 3  completes needed: 1  trust model: pgp
gpg: depth: 0  valid:   1  signed:   6  trust: 0-, 0q, 0n, 0m, 0f, 1u
gpg: depth: 1  valid:   6  signed:   0  trust: 6-, 0q, 0n, 0m, 0f, 0u
</span></code></pre></div>    </div>
  </li>
  <li>
    <p>Verify signed Git tags.</p>

    <div class="language-shell_session highlighter-rouge"><div class="highlight"><pre class="highlight"><code><span class="gp">$</span><span class="w"> </span><span class="nb">cd </span>qubes-secpack/
<span class="gp">$</span><span class="w"> </span>git tag <span class="nt">-v</span> <span class="sb">`</span>git describe<span class="sb">`</span>
<span class="go">object 266e14a6fae57c9a91362c9ac784d3a891f4d351
type commit
tag marmarek_sec_266e14a6
tagger Marek Marczykowski-Górecki 1677757924 +0100
   
Tag for commit 266e14a6fae57c9a91362c9ac784d3a891f4d351
gpg: Signature made Thu 02 Mar 2023 03:52:04 AM PST
gpg:                using RSA key 2D1771FE4D767EDC76B089FAD655A4F21830E06A
gpg: Good signature from "Marek Marczykowski-Górecki (Qubes security pack)" [full]
</span></code></pre></div>    </div>

    <p>The exact output will differ, but the final line should always start with <code class="language-plaintext highlighter-rouge">gpg: Good signature from...</code> followed by an appropriate key. The <code class="language-plaintext highlighter-rouge">[full]</code> indicates full trust, which this key inherits in virtue of being validly signed by the QMSK.</p>
  </li>
  <li>
    <p>Verify PGP signatures, e.g.:</p>

    <div class="language-shell_session highlighter-rouge"><div class="highlight"><pre class="highlight"><code><span class="gp">$</span><span class="w"> </span><span class="nb">cd </span>QSBs/
<span class="gp">$</span><span class="w"> </span>gpg <span class="nt">--verify</span> qsb-087-2022.txt.sig.marmarek qsb-087-2022.txt
<span class="go">gpg: Signature made Wed 23 Nov 2022 04:05:51 AM PST
gpg:                using RSA key 2D1771FE4D767EDC76B089FAD655A4F21830E06A
gpg: Good signature from "Marek Marczykowski-Górecki (Qubes security pack)" [full]
</span><span class="gp">$</span><span class="w"> </span>gpg <span class="nt">--verify</span> qsb-087-2022.txt.sig.simon qsb-087-2022.txt
<span class="go">gpg: Signature made Wed 23 Nov 2022 03:50:42 AM PST
gpg:                using RSA key EA18E7F040C41DDAEFE9AA0F4AC18DE1112E1490
gpg: Good signature from "Simon Gaiser (Qubes Security Pack signing key)" [full]
</span><span class="gp">$</span><span class="w"> </span><span class="nb">cd</span> ../canaries/
<span class="gp">$</span><span class="w"> </span>gpg <span class="nt">--verify</span> canary-034-2023.txt.sig.marmarek canary-034-2023.txt
<span class="go">gpg: Signature made Thu 02 Mar 2023 03:51:48 AM PST
gpg:                using RSA key 2D1771FE4D767EDC76B089FAD655A4F21830E06A
gpg: Good signature from "Marek Marczykowski-Górecki (Qubes security pack)" [full]
</span><span class="gp">$</span><span class="w"> </span>gpg <span class="nt">--verify</span> canary-034-2023.txt.sig.simon canary-034-2023.txt
<span class="go">gpg: Signature made Thu 02 Mar 2023 01:47:52 AM PST
gpg:                using RSA key EA18E7F040C41DDAEFE9AA0F4AC18DE1112E1490
gpg: Good signature from "Simon Gaiser (Qubes Security Pack signing key)" [full]
</span></code></pre></div>    </div>

    <p>Again, the exact output will differ, but the final line of output from each <code class="language-plaintext highlighter-rouge">gpg --verify</code> command should always start with <code class="language-plaintext highlighter-rouge">gpg: Good signature from...</code> followed by an appropriate key.</p>
  </li>
</ol>

<p>For this announcement (QSB-088), the commands are:</p>

<div class="language-plaintext highlighter-rouge"><div class="highlight"><pre class="highlight"><code>$ gpg --verify qsb-088-2023.txt.sig.marmarek qsb-088-2023.txt
$ gpg --verify qsb-088-2023.txt.sig.simon qsb-088-2023.txt
</code></pre></div></div>

<p>You can also verify the signatures directly from this announcement in addition to or instead of verifying the files from the qubes-secpack. Simply copy and paste the QSB-088 text into a plain text file and do the same for both signature files. Then, perform the same authentication steps as listed above, substituting the filenames above with the names of the files you just created.</p>

<hr>
<small>This is a companion discussion topic for the original entry at <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/news/2023/03/21/qsb-088/">https://www.qubes-os.org/news/2023/03/21/qsb-088/</a></small>

## XSAs released on 2023-03-21

> 板块: News

<p>The <a href="https://xenproject.org/">Xen Project</a> has released one or more <a href="https://xenbits.xen.org/xsa/">Xen security advisories (XSAs)</a>.
The security of Qubes OS <strong>is affected</strong>.
Therefore, <strong>user action is required</strong>.</p>

<h2 id="xsas-that-do-affect-the-security-of-qubes-os">XSAs that DO affect the security of Qubes OS</h2>

<p>The following XSAs <strong>do affect</strong> the security of Qubes OS:</p>

<ul>
  <li>XSA-428</li>
  <li>XSA-429</li>
</ul>

<p>Please see <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/news/2023/03/21/qsb-088/">QSB-088</a> for further details.</p>

<h2 id="xsas-that-do-not-affect-the-security-of-qubes-os">XSAs that DO NOT affect the security of Qubes OS</h2>

<p>The following XSAs <strong>do not affect</strong> the security of Qubes OS, and no user action is necessary:</p>

<ul>
  <li>XSA-427 (Shadow paging is disabled at build time.)</li>
</ul>

<h2 id="about-this-announcement">About this announcement</h2>

<p>Qubes OS uses the <a href="https://wiki.xenproject.org/wiki/Xen_Project_Software_Overview">Xen hypervisor</a> as part of its <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/doc/architecture/">architecture</a>. When the <a href="https://xenproject.org/">Xen Project</a> publicly discloses a vulnerability in the Xen hypervisor, they issue a notice called a <a href="https://xenproject.org/developers/security-policy/">Xen security advisory (XSA)</a>. Vulnerabilities in the Xen hypervisor sometimes have security implications for Qubes OS. When they do, we issue a notice called a <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/security/qsb/">Qubes security bulletin (QSB)</a>. (QSBs are also issued for non-Xen vulnerabilities.) However, QSBs can provide only <em>positive</em> confirmation that certain XSAs <em>do</em> affect the security of Qubes OS. QSBs cannot provide <em>negative</em> confirmation that other XSAs do <em>not</em> affect the security of Qubes OS. Therefore, we also maintain an <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/security/xsa/">XSA tracker</a>, which is a comprehensive list of all XSAs publicly disclosed to date, including whether each one affects the security of Qubes OS. When new XSAs are published, we add them to the XSA tracker and publish a notice like this one in order to inform Qubes users that a new batch of XSAs has been released and whether each one affects the security of Qubes OS.</p>

<hr>
<small>This is a companion discussion topic for the original entry at <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/news/2023/03/21/xsas-released-on-2023-03-21/">https://www.qubes-os.org/news/2023/03/21/xsas-released-on-2023-03-21/</a></small>

---

## Marek Marczykowski-Górecki to be interviewed at Dasharo virtual event

> 板块: News

<p>Our project lead, <a href="/team/#marek-marczykowski-górecki">Marek Marczykowski-Górecki</a> will be interviewed tomorrow during the <a href="https://vpub.dasharo.com/e/1/dasharo-user-group-1">Dasharo Developers vPub</a>. This is a virtual event hosted by the <a href="https://www.dasharo.com/">Dasharo</a> team, who just introduced <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/news/2023/03/15/dasharo-fidelisguard-z690-first-qubes-certified-desktop">the first Qubes-certified desktop computer</a>.</p>

<p><a href="https://vpub.dasharo.com/e/1/dasharo-user-group-1"><img src="https://www.qubes-os.org/attachment/posts/dasharo-event-1.png" alt="Dasharo User Group (DUG) #1 and Dasharo Developers vPub 0x6 informational poster"></a></p>

<p>The Dasharo Developers vPub will be preceded by the first Dasharo User Group meeting, which may be of interest for Qubes users who wish to learn more about open-source firmware or are curious about the <a href="https://3mdeb.com/shop/open-source-hardware/dasharo-fidelisguard-z690-qubes-os-certified/">Dasharo FidelisGuard Z690</a> Qubes-certified computer.</p>

<p><a href="https://vpub.dasharo.com/e/1/dasharo-user-group-1">Read the full announcement for more information.</a></p>

<h2 id="about-dasharo">About Dasharo</h2>

<p>“Dasharo is an open-source firmware distribution focusing on seamless deployment, clean and simple code, long-term maintenance, professional support, transparent validation, superior documentation, privacy-respecting implementation, liberty for the owners and trustworthiness for all.” <a href="https://docs.dasharo.com/osf-trivia-list/dasharo/">Learn more about Dasharo.</a></p>

<p>Dasharo is a registered trademark of and a product developed by <a href="https://3mdeb.com/">3mdeb</a>.</p>

<h2 id="about-3mdeb">About 3mdeb</h2>

<p>3mdeb and the Qubes OS Project have been partnering together for years to hold Qubes OS Summits. Michał Żygowski shared the story with us in <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/news/2022/09/07/qubes-os-summit-history/">Qubes OS Summit: History from organizer’s perspective</a>. You can watch videos from the 2022 summit <a href="https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=hkWWz3xGqS8">here</a> and <a href="https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=A9GrlQsQc7Q">here</a>. 3mdeb has also been instrumental in recent work on <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/news/2023/01/31/trenchboot-aem-for-qubes-os/">TrenchBoot Anti Evil Maid for Qubes OS</a>. <a href="https://3mdeb.com/about-us/">Learn more about 3mdeb.</a></p>

<hr>
<small>This is a companion discussion topic for the original entry at <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/news/2023/03/15/marek-marczykowski-gorecki-interviewed-dasharo-virtual-event/">https://www.qubes-os.org/news/2023/03/15/marek-marczykowski-gorecki-interviewed-dasharo-virtual-event/</a></small>

## Qubes OS 4.1.2 has been released!

> 板块: News

<p>We’re pleased to announce the stable release of Qubes 4.1.2! This release aims to consolidate all the security patches, bug fixes, and upstream template OS upgrades that have occurred since the initial Qubes 4.1.0 release. Our goal is to provide a secure and convenient way for users to install (or reinstall) the latest stable Qubes release with an up-to-date ISO.</p>

<p>Qubes 4.1.2 is available on the <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/downloads/">downloads</a> page.</p>

<h2 id="existing-installations">Existing installations</h2>

<p>If you are already using any version of Qubes 4.1 (including 4.1.0, 4.1.1, 4.1.2-rc1, and 4.1.2-rc2), then you should simply <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/doc/how-to-update/">update normally</a> (which includes <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/doc/how-to-update/#upgrading-to-avoid-eol">upgrading any EOL templates</a> you might have) in order to make your system effectively equivalent to this stable Qubes 4.1.2 release. No reinstallation or other special action is required.</p>

<h2 id="new-installations">New installations</h2>

<p>If you would like to install Qubes OS for the first time or perform a clean reinstallation on an existing system, there has never been a better time to do so! Simply <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/downloads/">download</a> the Qubes 4.1.2 ISO and follow our <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/doc/installation-guide/">installation guide</a>.</p>

<h2 id="whats-new-in-qubes-412">What’s new in Qubes 4.1.2?</h2>

<p>Qubes 4.1.2 includes numerous updates over the initial 4.1.0 release, in particular:</p>

<ul>
  <li>All 4.1 dom0 updates to date</li>
  <li>Fedora 37 template</li>
  <li>USB keyboard support in the installer (<a href="https://github.com/QubesOS/qubes-issues/issues/7674">#7674</a>)</li>
  <li><code class="language-plaintext highlighter-rouge">kernel-latest</code> available as a boot option when starting the installer (<a href="https://github.com/QubesOS/qubes-issues/issues/5900">#5900</a>)</li>
</ul>

<h2 id="what-is-a-patch-release">What is a patch release?</h2>

<p>The Qubes OS Project uses the <a href="https://semver.org/">semantic versioning</a> standard. Version numbers are written as <code class="language-plaintext highlighter-rouge"><major>.<minor>.<patch></patch></minor></major></code>. Hence, we refer to releases that increment the third number as “patch releases.” A patch release does not designate a separate, new major or minor release of Qubes OS. Rather, it designates its respective major or minor release (in this case, 4.1) inclusive of all updates up to a certain point. (See <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/doc/supported-releases/">supported releases</a> for a comprehensive list of major and minor releases.) Installing any prior Qubes 4.1 release and fully <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/doc/how-to-update/">updating</a> it results in essentially the same system as installing Qubes 4.1.2. You can learn more about how Qubes release versioning works in the <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/doc/version-scheme/">version scheme</a> documentation.</p>

<h2 id="reminder-qubes-40-has-reached-end-of-life">Reminder: Qubes 4.0 has reached end-of-life</h2>

<p>Qubes 4.0 <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/news/2022/07/04/qubes-os-4-0-eol-on-2022-08-04/">reached EOL (end-of-life) on 2022-08-04</a>. If you’re still using Qubes 4.0, we strongly recommend upgrading to Qubes 4.1.</p>

<hr>
<small>This is a companion discussion topic for the original entry at <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/news/2023/03/15/qubes-4-1-2/">https://www.qubes-os.org/news/2023/03/15/qubes-4-1-2/</a></small>

---

## Qubes Canary 034

> 板块: News

<p><em><strong>Editor’s note:</strong> An earlier version of this post mistakenly contained the text of an older canary. This has been corrected below. As always, we encourage readers to verify the cryptographic signatures on canaries, which can always be found in the <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/security/pack/">Qubes security pack (qubes-secpack)</a>.</em></p>

<p>We have published a new <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/security/canary/">Qubes canary</a>. The text of this canary is reproduced below. This canary and its accompanying cryptographic signatures will always be available in the <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/security/pack/">Qubes security pack (qubes-secpack)</a>.</p>

<div class="language-plaintext highlighter-rouge"><div class="highlight"><pre class="highlight"><code>
                    ---===[ Qubes Canary 034 ]===---


Statements
-----------

The Qubes security team members who have digitally signed this file [1]
state the following:

1. The date of issue of this canary is March 02, 2023.

2. There have been 87 Qubes security bulletins published so far.

3. The Qubes Master Signing Key fingerprint is:

       427F 11FD 0FAA 4B08 0123  F01C DDFA 1A3E 3687 9494

4. No warrants have ever been served to us with regard to the Qubes OS
   Project (e.g. to hand out the private signing keys or to introduce
   backdoors).

5. We plan to publish the next of these canary statements in the last
   fourteen days of May 2023. Special note should be taken if no new
   canary is published by that time or if the list of statements changes
   without plausible explanation.


Special announcements
----------------------

None.


Disclaimers and notes
----------------------

We would like to remind you that Qubes OS has been designed under the
assumption that all relevant infrastructure is permanently compromised.
This means that we assume NO trust in any of the servers or services
which host or provide any Qubes-related data, in particular, software
updates, source code repositories, and Qubes ISO downloads.

This canary scheme is not infallible. Although signing the declaration
makes it very difficult for a third party to produce arbitrary
declarations, it does not prevent them from using force or other means,
like blackmail or compromising the signers' laptops, to coerce us to
produce false declarations.

The proof of freshness provided below serves to demonstrate that this
canary could not have been created prior to the date stated. It shows
that a series of canaries was not created in advance.

This declaration is merely a best effort and is provided without any
guarantee or warranty. It is not legally binding in any way to anybody.
None of the signers should be ever held legally responsible for any of
the statements made here.

-------------------

## Qubes OS 4.1.2-rc2 has been released!

> 板块: News

<p>We’re pleased to announce the second <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org#what-is-a-release-candidate">release candidate</a> for Qubes 4.1.2! This <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org#what-is-a-patch-release">patch release</a> aims to consolidate all the security patches, bug fixes, and upstream template OS upgrades that have occurred since prior Qubes 4.1 releases. Our goal is to provide a secure and convenient way for users to install (or reinstall) the latest stable Qubes release with an up-to-date ISO.</p>

<p>Qubes 4.1.2-rc2 is available on the <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/downloads/">downloads</a> page.</p>

<h2 id="whats-new-in-qubes-412">What’s new in Qubes 4.1.2?</h2>

<p>Qubes 4.1.2-rc2 includes numerous updates over the initial 4.1.0 release, in particular:</p>

<ul>
  <li>All 4.1 dom0 updates to date</li>
  <li>Fedora 37 template</li>
  <li>USB keyboard support in the installer (<a href="https://github.com/QubesOS/qubes-issues/issues/7674">#7674</a>)</li>
  <li><code class="language-plaintext highlighter-rouge">kernel-latest</code> available as a boot option when starting the installer (<a href="https://github.com/QubesOS/qubes-issues/issues/5900">#5900</a>)</li>
  <li>Fixes for bugs discovered in 4.1.2-rc1</li>
</ul>

<h2 id="testing-qubes-412-rc2">Testing Qubes 4.1.2-rc2</h2>

<p>If you’re willing to <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/doc/testing/">test</a> this release candidate, you can help to improve the eventual stable release by <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/doc/issue-tracking/">reporting any bugs you encounter</a>. We strongly encourage experienced users to join the <a href="https://forum.qubes-os.org/t/joining-the-testing-team/5190">testing team</a>!</p>

<p>Thank you to everyone who tested and reported bugs in 4.1.2-rc1!</p>

<h2 id="existing-qubes-41-users">Existing Qubes 4.1 users</h2>

<p>If you’re not interested in testing this release candidate, and you’re already using Qubes 4.1, then you should simply <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/doc/how-to-update/">update normally</a> (which includes <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/doc/how-to-update/#upgrading-to-avoid-eol">upgrading any EOL templates</a> you might have) in order to make your system essentially equivalent to this patch release. No special action is required on your part.</p>

<h2 id="when-is-the-stable-release">When is the stable release?</h2>

<p>If no significant bugs are discovered in 4.1.2-rc2, we expect to announce the stable release of 4.1.2 in approximately one week.</p>

<h2 id="what-is-a-release-candidate">What is a release candidate?</h2>

<p>A release candidate (RC) is a software build that has the potential to become a stable release, unless significant bugs are discovered in testing. Release candidates are intended for more advanced (or adventurous!) users who are comfortable testing early versions of software that are potentially buggier than stable releases. You can read more about Qubes OS <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/doc/supported-releases/">supported releases</a> and the <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/doc/version-scheme/">version scheme</a> in our documentation.</p>

<h2 id="what-is-a-patch-release">What is a patch release?</h2>

<p>The Qubes OS Project uses the <a href="https://semver.org/">semantic versioning</a> standard. Version numbers are written as <code class="language-plaintext highlighter-rouge"><major>.<minor>.<patch></patch></minor></major></code>. Hence, we refer to releases that increment the third number as “patch releases.” A patch release does not designate a separate, new major or minor release of Qubes OS. Rather, it designates its respective major or minor release (in this case, 4.1) inclusive of all updates up to a certain point. (See <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/doc/supported-releases/">supported releases</a> for a comprehensive list of major and minor releases.) Installing any prior Qubes 4.1 release and fully <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/doc/how-to-update/">updating</a> it results in essentially the same system as installing Qubes 4.1.2. You can learn more about how Qubes release versioning works in the <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/doc/version-scheme/">version scheme</a> documentation.</p>

<hr>
<small>This is a companion discussion topic for the original entry at <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/news/2023/03/06/qubes-4-1-2-rc2/">https://www.qubes-os.org/news/2023/03/06/qubes-4-1-2-rc2/</a></small>

## Fedora 37 templates available

> 板块: News

<p>New Fedora 37 templates are now available! We provide fresh Fedora 37 template packages through the official Qubes repositories, which you can install in dom0 by following the standard <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/doc/templates/fedora/#installing">installation instructions</a>. Alternatively, we also provide step-by-step instructions for <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/doc/templates/fedora/in-place-upgrade/">performing an in-place upgrade</a> of an existing Fedora template. After upgrading your templates, please remember to <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/doc/templates/#switching">switch all qubes that were using the old template to use the new one</a>.</p>

<p>As a reminder, <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/news/2022/12/08/fedora-35-reaches-eol-on-2022-12-13/">Fedora 35 has reached EOL</a>. If you have not already done so, we strongly recommend that you <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/doc/templates/fedora/#upgrading">upgrade</a> all Fedora 35
templates and standalones to a <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/doc/supported-releases/#templates">supported template release</a> immediately.</p>

<p>Please note that no user action is required regarding the OS version in dom0 (see our <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/doc/supported-releases/#note-on-dom0-and-eol">note on dom0 and EOL</a>).</p>

<hr>
<small>This is a companion discussion topic for the original entry at <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/news/2023/03/03/fedora-37-templates-available/">https://www.qubes-os.org/news/2023/03/03/fedora-37-templates-available/</a></small>

---

## TrenchBoot Anti Evil Maid for Qubes OS

> 板块: News

<p><em><strong>Editor’s note:</strong> The following is a guest post by Michal Zygowski from <a href="https://3mdeb.com/">3mdeb</a> on the work they’ve been doing to upgrade <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/doc/anti-evil-maid/">Anti Evil Maid (AEM)</a>. The original post can be found on the <a href="https://blog.3mdeb.com/2023/2023-01-31-trenchboot-aem-for-qubesos/">3mdeb blog</a>. This work was made possible through generous <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/donate/">donations</a> from the Qubes community via <a href="https://opencollective.com/qubes-os">OpenCollective</a>. We are immensely grateful to the Qubes community for your continued support and to 3mdeb for contributing this valuable work.</em></p>

<h2 id="abstract">Abstract</h2>

<p>Qubes OS Anti Evil Maid (AEM) software heavily depends on the
availability of the DRTM technologies to prevent the Evil Maid
attacks. However, the project has not evolved much since the
beginning of 2018 and froze on the support of TPM 1.2 with Intel TXT
in legacy boot mode (BIOS). In the post we show how existing
solution can be replaced with TrenchBoot and how one can install it
on the Qubes OS. Also the post will also briefly explain how
TrenchBoot opens the door for future TPM 2.0 and UEFI support for
AEM.</p>

<h2 id="introduction">Introduction</h2>

<p>As Qubes OS users, promoters, and developers, we understand how essential it is
to be aware of the latest developments in maintaining the security of your
favorite operating system. We’re excited to share our plans to integrate the
TrenchBoot Project into Qubes OS’s new Anti-Evil Maid (AEM) implementation. As
you may know, traditional firmware security measures like UEFI Secure Boot and
measured boot, even with a Static Root of Trust (SRT), may only sometimes be
enough to ensure a completely secure environment for your operating system.
Compromised firmware may allow for the injection of malicious software into
your system, making it difficult to detect. To overcome these limitations, many
silicon vendors have started implementing Dynamic Root of Trust (DRT)
technologies to establish a secure environment for operating system launch and
integrity measurements. We’re excited to take advantage of these advancements
through integration with the <a href="https://trenchboot.org/">TrenchBoot Project</a>.</p>

<p>The usage of DRT technologies like Intel Trusted Execution Technology (TXT) or
AMD Secure Startup is becoming more and more significant; for example, Dynamic
Root of Trust for Measurement (DRTM) requirements of <a href="https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/windows-hardware/design/device-experiences/oem-highly-secure#what-makes-a-secured-core-pc">Microsoft Secured Core PCs</a>.
DRTM has yet to find its place in open-source projects, but that gradually
changes. The demand for having firmware-independent Roots of Trust is
increasing, and projects that satisfy this demand are growing TrenchBoot is a
framework that allows individuals and projects to build security engines to
perform launch integrity actions for their systems. The framework builds upon
Boot Integrity Technologies (BITs) that establish one or more Roots of Trust
(RoT) from which a degree of confidence that integrity actions were not
subverted.</p>

<p><a href="https://blog.invisiblethings.org/2011/09/07/anti-evil-maid.html">Qubes OS Anti Evil Maid (AEM)</a>
software heavily depends on the availability of DRTM technologies to prevent
Evil Maid attacks. However, the project hasn’t evolved much since the beginning
of 2018 and froze on the support of TPM 1.2 with Intel TXT in legacy boot mode
(BIOS). Because of that, the usage of this security software is effectively
limited to older Intel machines only. TPM 1.2 implemented SHA1 hashing
algorithm, which is nowadays considered weak in the era of forever-increasing
computer performance and quantum computing. The solution to this problem comes
with a newer TPM 2.0 with more agile cryptographic algorithms and SHA256
implementation by default.</p>

<p>The post will present the TrenchBoot solution for Qubes OS AEM replacing the
current TPM 1.2 and Intel TXT-only implementation. The advantage of TrenchBoot
solution over existing <a href="https://sourceforge.net/p/tboot/wiki/Home/">Trusted Boot</a>
is the easier future integration of AMD platform support, as well as TPM 2.0
and UEFI mode support.</p>

<p>Before we dive into the technical details, it is important to highlight that
this achievement was made possible through the generous contributions of Qubes
OS community via OpenCollective. We would like to express our gratitude and
extend a special thank you to all who have supported our favourite operating
system. To continue supporting Qubes OS, please consider donating through
<a href="https://opencollective.com/qubes-os">OpenCollective page</a>. Thank you for your
continued support!</p>

<h2 id="modificationts-to-original-qubes-os-aem">Modificationts to original Qubes OS AEM</h2>

<p>To replace the original implementation of Qubes OS AE
there weren’t any AEM scripts modifications necessary. What actually had to
change is GRUB and Xen Hypervisor (and Trusted Boot - to be removed). Why? one
may ask… First of all, one must understand the role of a Trusted Boot
(TBOOT).</p>

<h2 id="trusted-boot-drtm-flow">Trusted Boot DRTM flow</h2>

<p><a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/attachment/posts/txt_launch2.jpg"><img src="https://www.qubes-os.org/attachment/posts/txt_launch2.jpg" alt="Breakout of measured launch details"></a></p>

<p>(Source: <a href="https://link.springer.com/book/10.1007/978-1-4302-6584-9">A Practical Guide to TPM 2.0</a>)</p>

<p>The main role of Trusted Boot was to prepare a platform to be launched with
Intel TXT (Intel’s DRTM technology) in an operating system agnostic way. It has
been achieved by loading a tboot kernel with Multiboot protocol and the other
system components as the modules. That way, TBOOT is the main kernel that
starts first and prepares the platform for TXT launch. When the platform is
ready, then tboot performs the TXT launch. The control is passed to SINIT
Authenticated Code Module (ACM), a binary signed and provided by Intel designed
for DRTM technology. SINIT ACM uses TXT to measure the operating system
components in a secure manner. Then the control is handed back to the tboot
kernel, which checks if the operation was successful and boots the target
operating system.</p>

<p>Although the tboot tried to be as OS agnostic as possible, some tboot presence
awareness from the operating system is needed because the application processor
cores (all cores except the main one) are left in a special state after TXT
launch and cannot be woken up like in traditional boot process. To solve this
problem, tboot installs a special processor wakeup procedure in the memory,
which OS must call into to start the processor cores. Only then OS may
initialize the processor per its own requirements.</p>

<p>As one can see, the process is complex in the case of Intel TXT. Migration of
all tboot responsibilities was not trivial and has been divided into the work
on both GRUB and Xen Hypervisor side of Qubes OS.</p>

<h2 id="grub-modifications">GRUB modifications</h2>

<p><a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/attachment/posts/grub_logo.png"><img src="https://www.qubes-os.org/attachment/posts/grub_logo.png" alt="GRUB logo"></a></p>

<p>In order to fulfill the same role as tboot, GRUB had to learn how to prepare
the platform and perform the TXT launch. Most of the work for that particular
part has been done by <a href="https://www.mail-archive.com/grub-devel@gnu.org/msg30167.html">Oracle Team working on TrenchBoot for GRUB</a>.
That work, however, covered the Linux kernel TXT launch only. What still had to
be done was the Multiboot2 protocol support in GRUB to be able to TXT launch a
Xen Hypervisor. The patches have been prepared for the respective Qubes GRUB
<a href="https://github.com/3mdeb/qubes-grub2/pull/2">package</a>.</p>

<h2 id="xen-modifications">Xen modifications</h2>

<p><a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/attachment/posts/xen_project_logo.png"><img src="https://www.qubes-os.org/attachment/posts/xen_project_logo.png" alt="Xen Project logo"></a></p>

<p>Analogically to GRUB, Xen had to take over some responsibilities from tboot.
Due to the Intel TXT requirements for the boot process, a new entry point had
to be developed to which SINIT ACM will return control. The new entry point was
responsible for saving information that a TXT launch happened and cleaning up
the processor state so that the booting of the Xen kernel could continue with
the standard Multiboot2 path. Among others, if Xen detected TXT launch, it had
to perform the special processor cores wakeup process (which has been rewritten
from TrenchBoot Linux patches to Xen native code) and measure external
components before using them (that is the Xen parameters, Dom0 Linux kernel,
initrd and Dom0 parameters). Xen also had to reserve the memory regions used by
Intel TXT, as when tboot was used. The relevant source code for the respective
Qubes Xen package is available <a href="https://github.com/3mdeb/qubes-vmm-xen/pull/1">here</a>.</p>

<h2 id="installation-and-verification-of-trenchboot-aem-on-qubes-os">Installation and verification of TrenchBoot AEM on Qubes OS</h2>

<p>For a seamless deployment and installation of TrenchBoot AEM, the modifications
Qubes OS components compilation. Those patches have been presented earlier with
have been converted to patches which are applied to projects’ sources during
links to Pull Requests. It allows building ready-to-use RPM packages that can
be installed directly on an installed Qubes OS system. The pre-built packages
can be downloaded from <a href="https://3mdeb.com/open-source-firmware/QubesOS/trenchboot_aem_poc/">here</a>.
The packages have been covered with SHA512 sums signed with 3mdeb’s
<code class="language-plaintext highlighter-rouge">Qubes OS TrenchBoot AEM open-source software release 0.x signing key</code>
available on <a href="https://github.com/3mdeb/3mdeb-secpack/blob/master/open-source-software/qubes-os-trenchboot-aem-open-source-software-release-0.x-signing-key.asc">3mdeb-secpack repository</a>.
To verify the RPM packages, fetch the key with the following command:
<code class="language-plaintext highlighter-rouge">gpg --fetch https://raw.githubusercontent.com/3mdeb/3mdeb-secpack/master/open-source-software/qubes-os-trenchboot-aem-open-source-software-release-0.x-signing-key.asc</code>
and then to verify the packages, please run:</p>

<div class="language-bash highlighter-rouge"><div class="highlight"><pre class="highlight"><code><span class="nv">$ </span>gpg <span class="nt">--verify</span> sha512sums.sig sha512sums

gpg: Signature made wto, 31 sty 2023, 11:06:06 CET
gpg:                using RSA key 3405D1E4509CD18A3EA762245D289020C07114F3
gpg: Good signature from <span class="s2">"Qubes OS TrenchBoot AEM open-source software release 0.x signing key"</span> <span class="o">[</span>unknown]
gpg: WARNING: This key is not certified with a trusted signature!
gpg:          There is no indication that the signature belongs to the owner.
Primary key fingerprint: 3405 D1E4 509C D18A 3EA7  6224 5D28 9020 C071 14F3

<span class="nv">$ </span><span class="nb">sha512sum</span> <span class="nt">-c</span> sha512sums

grub2-common-2.06-1.fc32.noarch.rpm: OK
grub2-tools-extra-2.06-1.fc32.x86_64.rpm: OK
xen-licenses-4.17.0-3.fc32.x86_64.rpm: OK
grub2-pc-2.06-1.fc32.x86_64.rpm: OK
xen-libs-4.17.0-3.fc32.x86_64.rpm: OK
grub2-tools-2.06-1.fc32.x86_64.rpm: OK
xen-hypervisor-4.17.0-3.fc32.x86_64.rpm: OK
grub2-pc-modules-2.06-1.fc32.noarch.rpm: OK
xen-runtime-4.17.0-3.fc32.x86_64.rpm: OK
grub2-tools-minimal-2.06-1.fc32.x86_64.rpm: OK
python3-xen-4.17.0-3.fc32.x86_64.rpm: OK
xen-4.17.0-3.fc32.x86_64.rpm: OK
</code></pre></div></div>

<p>Check if GPG returns a good signature and if yes, check if the RPM checksum
matches. All files must be in the same directory for the procedure to work.</p>

<p>Note, in order to use the TrenchBoot AEM for Qubes OS, you have to own a
TXT-capable platform with TXT-enabled firmware offering legacy boot. Such
platform can be Dell OptiPlex 7010. You can visit
<a href="https://blog.3mdeb.com/2022/2022-03-17-optiplex-txt/">Dasharo with Intel TXT support blog post</a>
to learn more about such hardware and firmware. If you want to get OptiPlex
with Dasharo pre-installed, you can get one from
<a href="https://3mdeb.com/shop/open-source-hardware/dasharo-dell-optiplex-7010-sff-i3-i7-8gb-32gb-ram-copy/">3mdeb shop</a>.</p>

<h2 id="building-xen-and-grub-packages">Building Xen and GRUB packages</h2>

<p>If you are not interested in compilation, skip to the <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org#installing-xen-and-grub-packages">next section</a>.</p>

<p>To not make the post excessively long, the procedure for building packages
has been put into <a href="https://github.com/TrenchBoot/trenchboot-sdk/blob/3d56ca7b27bb038629fd838819a1050006725a1e/Documentation/build_qubes_packages.md">TrenchBoot SDK documentation</a>.
Follow the instructions in the file to build the TrenchBoot AEM packages.</p>

<h2 id="installing-xen-and-grub-packages">Installing Xen and GRUB packages</h2>

<p>The following process was carried out and tested on
<a href="https://openqa.qubes-os.org/tests/55506#downloads">Qubes OS 4.2</a>.</p>

<p>In order to install the packages one has to send the Xen and GRUB RPMs to the
Dom0. Please note that moving any external files or data to Dom0 is potentially
dangerous. Ensure that your environment is safe and the RPMs have the right
checksums after copying them to Dom0. If you don’t know how to copy files to
Dom0, refer to the <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/doc/how-to-copy-from-dom0/#copying-to-dom0">Qubes OS documentation</a>.</p>

<ol>
  <li>
    <p>Even before installing packages, it is required to enable the
<code class="language-plaintext highlighter-rouge">current-testing</code> repository to avoid the need to install additional
dependencies:</p>

    <div class="language-bash highlighter-rouge"><div class="highlight"><pre class="highlight"><code> <span class="nb">sudo </span>qubes-dom0-update <span class="nt">--enablerepo</span><span class="o">=</span>qubes-dom0-current-testing
</code></pre></div>    </div>
  </li>
  <li>
    <p>If the RPMs are inside Dom0, install them with the following command
(assuming you downloaded all of them to one directory):</p>

    <div class="language-bash highlighter-rouge"><div class="highlight"><pre class="highlight"><code> <span class="nb">sudo </span>dnf update <span class="se">\</span>
     python3-xen-4.17.0-3.fc32.x86_64.rpm <span class="se">\</span>
     xen-4.17.0-3.fc32.x86_64.rpm <span class="se">\</span>
     xen-hypervisor-4.17.0-3.fc32.x86_64.rpm <span class="se">\</span>
     xen-libs-4.17.0-3.fc32.x86_64.rpm <span class="se">\</span>
     xen-licenses-4.17.0-3.fc32.x86_64.rpm <span class="se">\</span>
     xen-runtime-4.17.0-3.fc32.x86_64.rpm <span class="se">\</span>
     grub2-common-2.06-1.fc32.noarch.rpm <span class="se">\</span>
     grub2-pc-modules-2.06-1.fc32.noarch.rpm <span class="se">\</span>
     grub2-pc-2.06-1.fc32.x86_64.rpm <span class="se">\</span>
     grub2-tools-2.06-1.fc32.x86_64.rpm <span class="se">\</span>
     grub2-tools-extra-2.06-1.fc32.x86_64.rpm <span class="se">\</span>
     grub2-tools-minimal-2.06-1.fc32.x86_64.rpm
</code></pre></div>    </div>
  </li>
  <li>Invoke <code class="language-plaintext highlighter-rouge">sudo grub2-install /dev/sdX</code>, where X is the letter representing the
disk with <code class="language-plaintext highlighter-rouge">/boot</code> partition.</li>
  <li>Additionally, you will have to download SINIT ACM and place it in <code class="language-plaintext highlighter-rouge">/boot</code>
partition/directory so that GRUB will be able to pick it up. Note it is only
necessary if your firmware/BIOS does not include/place SINIT ACM in the
Intel TXT region. You may obtain all SINIT ACMs as described
<a href="https://github.com/QubesOS/qubes-antievilmaid/blob/7561a4d724b9b0df8ba48d8f2735d3754961f87b/README#L177">here</a>.
Copy the SINIT ACM suitable for your platform to <code class="language-plaintext highlighter-rouge">/boot</code> directory. In the
case of Dell OptiPlex it will be <code class="language-plaintext highlighter-rouge">SNB_IVB_SINIT_20190708_PW.bin</code>.</li>
  <li>
    <p>Install Qubes AEM packages with the following command because Qubes OS 4.2
lacks AEM packages:</p>

    <div class="language-bash highlighter-rouge"><div class="highlight"><pre class="highlight"><code> qubes-dom0-update  <span class="nt">--enablerepo</span><span class="o">=</span>qubes-dom0-current-testing anti-evil-maid
</code></pre></div>    </div>
  </li>
  <li>Enter the SeaBIOS TPM menu (hotkey <code class="language-plaintext highlighter-rouge">t</code>) and choose the clear TPM option.
Then activate and enable the TPM by selecting the appropriate options. If in
any case you are using proprietary firmware, clear the TPM and then enable
and activate it in the firmware setup application.</li>
  <li>Follow the steps in <a href="https://github.com/QubesOS/qubes-antievilmaid/blob/7561a4d724b9b0df8ba48d8f2735d3754961f87b/README#L147">set up TPM for AEM</a>.</li>
  <li>The anti-evil-maid script may not work with LUKS2 in its current state, so
make a fix according to this <a href="https://github.com/QubesOS/qubes-antievilmaid/pull/41/files">Pull Request</a>
if needed.</li>
  <li>Now it is possible to <a href="https://github.com/QubesOS/qubes-antievilmaid/blob/7561a4d724b9b0df8ba48d8f2735d3754961f87b/README#L202">setup Qubes OS AEM device</a>.
This will create the AEM entry in Qubes GRUB, but this entry is using tboot.</li>
  <li>
    <p>You will need to edit the grub configuration file (<code class="language-plaintext highlighter-rouge">/boot/grub2/grub.cfg</code>)
by copying the standard Qubes OS entry (without AEM) and adding:</p>

    <div class="language-bash highlighter-rouge"><div class="highlight"><pre class="highlight"><code>slaunch
slaunch_module /<name_of_the_sinit_acm>
</name_of_the_sinit_acm></code></pre></div>    </div>

    <p>before the <code class="language-plaintext highlighter-rouge">multiboot2</code> directive, which loads Xen Hypervisor. Name the
entry differently, e.g. <code class="language-plaintext highlighter-rouge">Qubes OS with TrenchBoot AEM</code>. Also, you will need
to copy the AEM parameters for the Linux kernel: e.g.:</p>

    <pre><code class="language-txt">aem.uuid=38474da6-7b2d-410d-95e6-8683005fb23f rd.luks.key=/tmp/aem-keyfile rd.luks.crypttab=no
</code></pre>
    <p>We are still working on automating this step, so please bear with the
manual file edition for now.</p>
  </li>
</ol>

<p>Example GRUB entry:</p>

<div class="language-bash highlighter-rouge"><div class="highlight"><pre class="highlight"><code>menuentry <span class="s1">'Qubes, with TrenchBoot AEM'</span> <span class="nt">--class</span> qubes <span class="nt">--class</span> gnu-linux <span class="nt">--class</span> gnu <span class="nt">--class</span> os <span class="nt">--class</span> xen <span class="nv">$menuentry_id_option</span> <span class="s1">'xen-gnulinux-simple-/dev/mapper/qubes_dom0-root'</span> <span class="o">{</span>
        insmod part_msdos
        insmod ext2
        <span class="nb">set </span><span class="nv">root</span><span class="o">=</span><span class="s1">'hd0,msdos1'</span>
        <span class="k">if</span> <span class="o">[</span> x<span class="nv">$feature_platform_search_hint</span> <span class="o">=</span> xy <span class="o">]</span><span class="p">;</span> <span class="k">then
          </span>search <span class="nt">--no-floppy</span> <span class="nt">--fs-uuid</span> <span class="nt">--set</span><span class="o">=</span>root <span class="nt">--hint-bios</span><span class="o">=</span>hd0,msdos1 <span class="nt">--hint-efi</span><span class="o">=</span>hd0,msdos1 <span class="nt">--hint-baremetal</span><span class="o">=</span>ahci0,msdos1 <span class="nt">--hint</span><span class="o">=</span><span class="s1">'hd0,msdos1'</span>  38474da6-7b2d-410d-95e6-8683005fb23f
        <span class="k">else
          </span>search <span class="nt">--no-floppy</span> <span class="nt">--fs-uuid</span> <span class="nt">--set</span><span class="o">=</span>root 38474da6-7b2d-410d-95e6-8683005fb23f
        <span class="k">fi
        </span><span class="nb">echo</span>    <span class="s1">'Loading Xen 4.17.0 ...'</span>
        <span class="k">if</span> <span class="o">[</span> <span class="s2">"</span><span class="nv">$grub_platform</span><span class="s2">"</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="s2">"pc"</span> <span class="nt">-o</span> <span class="s2">"</span><span class="nv">$grub_platform</span><span class="s2">"</span> <span class="o">=</span> <span class="s2">""</span> <span class="o">]</span><span class="p">;</span> <span class="k">then
            </span><span class="nv">xen_rm_opts</span><span class="o">=</span>
        <span class="k">else
            </span><span class="nv">xen_rm_opts</span><span class="o">=</span><span class="s2">"no-real-mode edd=off"</span>
        <span class="k">fi
        </span>slaunch
        slaunch_module  /SNB_IVB_SINIT_20190708_PW.bin
        multiboot2      /xen-4.17.0.gz placeholder  <span class="nv">console</span><span class="o">=</span>none <span class="nv">dom0_mem</span><span class="o">=</span>min:1024M <span class="nv">dom0_mem</span><span class="o">=</span>max:4096M <span class="nv">ucode</span><span class="o">=</span>scan <span class="nv">smt</span><span class="o">=</span>off <span class="nv">gnttab_max_frames</span><span class="o">=</span>2048 <span class="nv">gnttab_max_maptrack_frames</span><span class="o">=</span>4096 <span class="k">${</span><span class="nv">xen_rm_opts</span><span class="k">}</span>
        <span class="nb">echo</span>    <span class="s1">'Loading Linux 5.15.81-1.fc32.qubes.x86_64 ...'</span>
        module  /vmlinuz-5.15.81-1.fc32.qubes.x86_64 placeholder <span class="nv">root</span><span class="o">=</span>/dev/mapper/qubes_dom0-root ro rd.luks.uuid<span class="o">=</span>luks-f1f850fa-59bf-4911-8256-4986c485e112 rd.lvm.lv<span class="o">=</span>qubes_dom0/root rd.lvm.lv<span class="o">=</span>qubes_dom0/swap i915.alpha_support<span class="o">=</span>1 rd.driver.pre<span class="o">=</span>btrfs rhgb quiet <span class="nv">console</span><span class="o">=</span>ttyS0,115200  aem.uuid<span class="o">=</span>38474da6-7b2d-410d-95e6-8683005fb23f rd.luks.key<span class="o">=</span>/tmp/aem-keyfile rd.luks.crypttab<span class="o">=</span>no
        <span class="nb">echo</span>    <span class="s1">'Loading initial ramdisk ...'</span>
        module2 <span class="nt">--nounzip</span>   /initramfs-5.15.81-1.fc32.qubes.x86_64.img
<span class="o">}</span>
</code></pre></div></div>

<p>You may put the above entry to <code class="language-plaintext highlighter-rouge">/etc/grub.d/40_custom</code> to avoid the removal of
the TrenchBoot AEM entry in case <code class="language-plaintext highlighter-rouge">grub2-mkconfig</code> will be called to overwrite
<code class="language-plaintext highlighter-rouge">grub.cfg</code>. Please note such a workaround will not allow for automatic Xen,
Linux, and initrd updates which may include security fixes. Making the
<code class="language-plaintext highlighter-rouge">grub.cfg</code> seamless for TrenchBoot is still in progress.</p>

<h2 id="verifying-trenchboot-aem-for-qubes-os">Verifying TrenchBoot AEM for Qubes OS</h2>

<p>The moment of truth has come. If the installation has been performed
successfully, it is time to try out the TXT launch. So reboot the platform and
choose the newly created entry with TrenchBoot. If it succeeds, you should get
a TPM SRK and LUKS password prompts.</p>

<p>After the system boots, one may check if DRTM PCRs (17 and 18, 19 is not used
by TrenchBoot for now) have been populated:</p>

<div class="language-plaintext highlighter-rouge"><div class="highlight"><pre class="highlight"><code>cat /sys/class/tpm/tpm0/pcrs
PCR-00: 3A 3F 78 0F 11 A4 B4 99 69 FC AA 80 CD 6E 39 57 C3 3B 22 75
PCR-01: 4D E4 B0 42 71 50 E4 B1 DE C0 D7 F1 A0 29 A2 65 11 30 72 FD
PCR-02: CE EA EC 0A 01 D5 7B A3 55 5A 4C 02 59 4A EE A1 C9 41 78 FB
PCR-03: 3A 3F 78 0F 11 A4 B4 99 69 FC AA 80 CD 6E 39 57 C3 3B 22 75
PCR-04: 01 7A 3D E8 2F 4A 1B 77 FC 33 A9 03 FE F6 AD 27 EE 92 BE 04
PCR-05: BF 4E 38 B0 A7 7A 7A 4D 1A A9 B5 0F 59 D8 E5 F7 A6 46 8E 48
PCR-06: 3A 3F 78 0F 11 A4 B4 99 69 FC AA 80 CD 6E 39 57 C3 3B 22 75
PCR-07: 3A 3F 78 0F 11 A4 B4 99 69 FC AA 80 CD 6E 39 57 C3 3B 22 75
PCR-08: 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00
PCR-09: 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00
PCR-10: 9A 51 66 4D EB 1C B9 72 91 87 59 C4 89 AC 9A FF 7F 10 BF B3
PCR-11: 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00
PCR-12: 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00
PCR-13: 10 78 D0 16 8C 85 85 3A 7E 0A A1 D7 56 02 A7 05 D4 7F 22 64
PCR-14: 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00
PCR-15: 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00
PCR-16: 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00
PCR-17: 2A C9 64 F2 E2 96 50 B3 1D B7 2F 77 C4 7C A6 5D AA C8 4E E7
PCR-18: 84 4D D5 8D 95 EB 96 F6 CE 92 51 9C FD E2 33 45 71 C3 87 92
PCR-19: 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00
PCR-20: 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00
PCR-21: 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00
PCR-22: 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00
PCR-23: 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00 00
</code></pre></div></div>

<p>Note that AEM will fail to unseal secrets as the PCRs are changed. To re-seal
the secret you will have to perform the following steps after a successful boot
with TrenchBoot:</p>

<ol>
  <li>Re-seal the secret <code class="language-plaintext highlighter-rouge">sudo anti-evil-maid-seal ""</code>.</li>
  <li>Reboot the machine and notice that the anti-evil-maid service no longer
fails during boot. The secret should be displayed on the screen, indicating
the machine boots correctly and unseals the secret.</li>
</ol>

<h2 id="summary">Summary</h2>

<p>It has been shown that TrenchBoot can be integrated to perform DRTM secure
launch of Qubes OS in place of old tboot. Moreover, TrenchBoot is more
extensible to other platforms like AMD. In the future, Anti Evil Maid will be
available on both Intel and AMD platforms with both TPM 1.2 and TPM 2.0, thanks
to TrenchBoot (which seemed to not be possible with tboot only).</p>

<p>If you think we can help in improving the security of your firmware or you are
looking for someone who can boost your product by leveraging advanced features
of used hardware platform, feel free to <a href="https://calendly.com/3mdeb/consulting-remote-meeting">book a call with us</a>
or drop us email to <code class="language-plaintext highlighter-rouge">contact<at>3mdeb<dot>com</dot></at></code>. If you are interested in
similar content, feel free to <a href="https://newsletter.3mdeb.com/subscription/PW6XnCeK6">sign up to our newsletter</a></p>

<hr>
<small>This is a companion discussion topic for the original entry at <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/news/2023/01/31/trenchboot-aem-for-qubes-os/">https://www.qubes-os.org/news/2023/01/31/trenchboot-aem-for-qubes-os/</a></small>

## XSAs released on 2023-02-14

> 板块: News

<p>The <a href="https://xenproject.org/">Xen Project</a> has released one or more <a href="https://xenbits.xen.org/xsa/">Xen security advisories (XSAs)</a>.
The security of Qubes OS <strong>is not affected</strong>.
Therefore, <strong>no user action is required</strong>.</p>

<h2 id="xsas-that-do-affect-the-security-of-qubes-os">XSAs that DO affect the security of Qubes OS</h2>

<p>The following XSAs <strong>do affect</strong> the security of Qubes OS:</p>

<ul>
  <li>(none)</li>
</ul>

<h2 id="xsas-that-do-not-affect-the-security-of-qubes-os">XSAs that DO NOT affect the security of Qubes OS</h2>

<p>The following XSAs <strong>do not affect</strong> the security of Qubes OS, and no user action is necessary:</p>

<ul>
  <li>XSA-426 (SMT is disabled in Qubes OS by default)</li>
</ul>

<h2 id="about-this-announcement">About this announcement</h2>

<p>Qubes OS uses the <a href="https://wiki.xenproject.org/wiki/Xen_Project_Software_Overview">Xen hypervisor</a> as part of its <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/doc/architecture/">architecture</a>. When the <a href="https://xenproject.org/">Xen Project</a> publicly discloses a vulnerability in the Xen hypervisor, they issue a notice called a <a href="https://xenproject.org/developers/security-policy/">Xen security advisory (XSA)</a>. Vulnerabilities in the Xen hypervisor sometimes have security implications for Qubes OS. When they do, we issue a notice called a <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/security/qsb/">Qubes security bulletin (QSB)</a>. (QSBs are also issued for non-Xen vulnerabilities.) However, QSBs can provide only <em>positive</em> confirmation that certain XSAs <em>do</em> affect the security of Qubes OS. QSBs cannot provide <em>negative</em> confirmation that other XSAs do <em>not</em> affect the security of Qubes OS. Therefore, we also maintain an <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/security/xsa/">XSA tracker</a>, which is a comprehensive list of all XSAs publicly disclosed to date, including whether each one affects the security of Qubes OS. When new XSAs are published, we add them to the XSA tracker and publish a notice like this one in order to inform Qubes users that a new batch of XSAs has been released and whether each one affects the security of Qubes OS.</p>

<hr>
<small>This is a companion discussion topic for the original entry at <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/news/2023/02/15/xsas-released-on-2023-02-14/">https://www.qubes-os.org/news/2023/02/15/xsas-released-on-2023-02-14/</a></small>

---

## Qubes OS 4.1.2-rc1 has been released!

> 板块: News

<p>We’re pleased to announce the first <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org#what-is-a-release-candidate">release candidate</a> for Qubes 4.1.2! This <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org#what-is-a-patch-release">patch release</a> aims to consolidate all the security patches, bug fixes, and upstream template OS upgrades that have occurred since prior Qubes 4.1 releases. Our goal is to provide a secure and convenient way for users to install (or reinstall) the latest stable Qubes release with an up-to-date ISO.</p>

<p>Qubes 4.1.2-rc1 is available on the <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/downloads/">downloads</a> page.</p>

<h2 id="whats-new-in-qubes-412">What’s new in Qubes 4.1.2?</h2>

<p>Qubes 4.1.2-rc1 includes numerous updates over the initial 4.1.0 release, in particular:</p>

<ul>
  <li>All 4.1 dom0 updates to date</li>
  <li>Fedora 37 template</li>
  <li>USB keyboard support in the installer (<a href="https://github.com/QubesOS/qubes-issues/issues/7674">#7674</a>)</li>
  <li><code class="language-plaintext highlighter-rouge">kernel-latest</code> available as a boot option when starting the installer (<a href="https://github.com/QubesOS/qubes-issues/issues/5900">#5900</a>)</li>
</ul>

<h2 id="testing-qubes-412-rc1">Testing Qubes 4.1.2-rc1</h2>

<p>If you’re willing to <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/doc/testing/">test</a> this release candidate, you can help to improve the eventual stable release by <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/doc/issue-tracking/">reporting any bugs you encounter</a>. We strongly encourage experienced users to join the <a href="https://forum.qubes-os.org/t/joining-the-testing-team/5190">testing team</a>!</p>

<h2 id="existing-qubes-41-users">Existing Qubes 4.1 users</h2>

<p>If you’re not interested in testing this release candidate, and you’re already using Qubes 4.1, then you should simply <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/doc/how-to-update/">update normally</a> (which includes <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/doc/how-to-update/#upgrading-to-avoid-eol">upgrading any EOL templates</a> you might have) in order to make your system essentially equivalent to this patch release. No special action is required on your part.</p>

<h2 id="release-candidate-planning">Release candidate planning</h2>

<p>If no significant bugs are discovered in 4.1.2-rc1, we expect to announce the stable release of 4.1.2 in two to three weeks.</p>

<h2 id="what-is-a-release-candidate">What is a release candidate?</h2>

<p>A release candidate (RC) is a software build that has the potential to become a stable release, unless significant bugs are discovered in testing. Release candidates are intended for more advanced (or adventurous!) users who are comfortable testing early versions of software that are potentially buggier than stable releases. You can read more about Qubes OS <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/doc/supported-releases/">supported releases</a> and the <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/doc/version-scheme/">version scheme</a> in our documentation.</p>

<h2 id="what-is-a-patch-release">What is a patch release?</h2>

<p>The Qubes OS Project uses the <a href="https://semver.org/">semantic versioning</a> standard. Version numbers are written as <code class="language-plaintext highlighter-rouge"><major>.<minor>.<patch></patch></minor></major></code>. Hence, we refer to releases that increment the third number as “patch releases.” A patch release does not designate a separate, new major or minor release of Qubes OS. Rather, it designates its respective major or minor release (in this case, 4.1) inclusive of all updates up to a certain point. (See <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/doc/supported-releases/">supported releases</a> for a comprehensive list of major and minor releases.) Installing any prior Qubes 4.1 release and fully <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/doc/how-to-update/">updating</a> it results in essentially the same system as installing Qubes 4.1.2. You can learn more about how Qubes release versioning works in the <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/doc/version-scheme/">version scheme</a> documentation.</p>

<hr>
<small>This is a companion discussion topic for the original entry at <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/news/2023/02/09/qubes-4-1-2-rc1/">https://www.qubes-os.org/news/2023/02/09/qubes-4-1-2-rc1/</a></small>

---

## XSAs released on 2023-01-25

> 板块: News

<p>The <a href="https://xenproject.org/">Xen Project</a> has released one or more <a href="https://xenbits.xen.org/xsa/">Xen security advisories (XSAs)</a>.
The security of Qubes OS <strong>is not affected</strong>.
Therefore, <strong>no user action is required</strong>.</p>

<h2 id="xsas-that-do-affect-the-security-of-qubes-os">XSAs that DO affect the security of Qubes OS</h2>

<p>The following XSAs <strong>do affect</strong> the security of Qubes OS:</p>

<ul>
  <li>(none)</li>
</ul>

<h2 id="xsas-that-do-not-affect-the-security-of-qubes-os">XSAs that DO NOT affect the security of Qubes OS</h2>

<p>The following XSAs <strong>do not affect</strong> the security of Qubes OS, and no user action is necessary:</p>

<ul>
  <li>XSA-425 (Qubes 4.1 does not use the affected Xen version; denial-of-service only)</li>
</ul>

<h2 id="about-this-announcement">About this announcement</h2>

<p>Qubes OS uses the <a href="https://wiki.xenproject.org/wiki/Xen_Project_Software_Overview">Xen hypervisor</a> as part of its <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/doc/architecture/">architecture</a>. When the <a href="https://xenproject.org/">Xen Project</a> publicly discloses a vulnerability in the Xen hypervisor, they issue a notice called a <a href="https://xenproject.org/developers/security-policy/">Xen security advisory (XSA)</a>. Vulnerabilities in the Xen hypervisor sometimes have security implications for Qubes OS. When they do, we issue a notice called a <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/security/qsb/">Qubes security bulletin (QSB)</a>. (QSBs are also issued for non-Xen vulnerabilities.) However, QSBs can provide only <em>positive</em> confirmation that certain XSAs <em>do</em> affect the security of Qubes OS. QSBs cannot provide <em>negative</em> confirmation that other XSAs do <em>not</em> affect the security of Qubes OS. Therefore, we also maintain an <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/security/xsa/">XSA tracker</a>, which is a comprehensive list of all XSAs publicly disclosed to date, including whether each one affects the security of Qubes OS. When new XSAs are published, we add them to the XSA tracker and publish a notice like this one in order to inform Qubes users that a new batch of XSAs has been released and whether each one affects the security of Qubes OS.</p>

<hr>
<small>This is a companion discussion topic for the original entry at <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/news/2023/01/27/xsas-released-on-2023-01-25/">https://www.qubes-os.org/news/2023/01/27/xsas-released-on-2023-01-25/</a></small>

---

## Support the Qubes OS Project via Proton's charity fundraiser!

> 板块: News

<p>The Qubes OS Project is grateful to have been selected as one of the beneficiaries of this year’s <a href="https://proton.me/">Proton</a> charity fundraiser alongside so many other wonderful organizations. The continued support of the privacy community means the world to us! For details about the fundraiser and how you can participate, please see the official Proton blog post: <a href="https://proton.me/blog/2022-lifetime-account-charity-fundraiser">The 2022 Lifetime Account Charity Fundraiser has started!</a></p>

## Fedora 35 reaches EOL on 2022-12-13

> 板块: News

<p>The Fedora Project has <a href="https://lists.fedoraproject.org/archives/list/devel-announce@lists.fedoraproject.org/thread/OGTVKLX7OXBYCEUQ66UY4YK3T6QHAYW5/">announced</a> that Fedora 35 will reach EOL (<a href="https://fedoraproject.org/wiki/End_of_life">end-of-life</a>) on 2022-12-13. We strongly recommend that all users <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/doc/templates/fedora/#upgrading">upgrade</a> their Fedora templates and standalones to Fedora 36 no later than 2022-12-13.</p>

<p>We provide fresh Fedora 36 template packages through the official Qubes repositories, which you can install in dom0 by following the standard <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/doc/templates/fedora/#installing">installation instructions</a>. Alternatively, we also provide step-by-step instructions for <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/doc/templates/fedora/in-place-upgrade/">performing an in-place upgrade</a> of an existing Fedora template. After upgrading your templates, please remember to <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/doc/templates/#switching">switch all qubes that were using the old template to use the new one</a>.</p>

<p>For a complete list of template releases that are supported for your specific Qubes release, see our <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/doc/supported-releases/#templates">supported template releases</a>.</p>

<p>Please note that no user action is required regarding the OS version in dom0. For details, please see our <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/doc/supported-releases/#note-on-dom0-and-eol">note on dom0 and EOL</a>.</p>

<hr>
<small>This is a companion discussion topic for the original entry at <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/news/2022/12/08/fedora-35-reaches-eol-on-2022-12-13/">https://www.qubes-os.org/news/2022/12/08/fedora-35-reaches-eol-on-2022-12-13/</a></small>

---
