## XSAs 333, 334, 336, 338, 339, 342, and 344 do not affect the security of Qubes OS

> 板块: News

<p>The Xen Project has published the following Xen Security Advisories:
XSA-333, XSA-334, XSA-336, XSA-338, XSA-339, XSA-342, and XSA-344.
These XSAs do <strong>not</strong> affect the security of Qubes OS, and no user
action is necessary.</p>

<p>These XSAs have been added to the <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/security/xsa/">XSA Tracker</a>:</p>

<p><a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/security/xsa/#333">https://www.qubes-os.org/security/xsa/#333</a><br>
<a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/security/xsa/#334">https://www.qubes-os.org/security/xsa/#334</a><br>
<a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/security/xsa/#336">https://www.qubes-os.org/security/xsa/#336</a><br>
<a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/security/xsa/#338">https://www.qubes-os.org/security/xsa/#338</a><br>
<a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/security/xsa/#339">https://www.qubes-os.org/security/xsa/#339</a><br>
<a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/security/xsa/#342">https://www.qubes-os.org/security/xsa/#342</a><br>
<a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/security/xsa/#344">https://www.qubes-os.org/security/xsa/#344</a></p>


<hr>
<small>This is a companion discussion topic for the original entry at <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/news/2020/09/22/xsa-333-334-336-338-339-342-344-qubes-not-affected/">https://www.qubes-os.org/news/2020/09/22/xsa-333-334-336-338-339-342-344-qubes-not-affected/</a></small>

---

## Fedora 31 approaching EOL

> 板块: News

<p>Fedora 33 was <a href="https://fedoramagazine.org/announcing-fedora-33/">released today</a>, 2020-10-27. According to the <a href="https://fedoraproject.org/wiki/Fedora_Release_Life_Cycle">Fedora
Release Life Cycle</a>, this means that Fedora 31 is scheduled to reach
EOL (<a href="https://fedoraproject.org/wiki/End_of_life">end-of-life</a>) in approximately four weeks, around <a href="https://www.timeanddate.com/date/dateadded.html?m1=10&amp;d1=27&amp;y1=2020&amp;type=add&amp;ay=&amp;am=&amp;aw=4&amp;ad=&amp;rec=">2020-11-24</a>.</p>

<p>We strongly recommend that all Qubes users upgrade their Fedora 31
TemplateVMs and StandaloneVMs to Fedora 32 or higher before Fedora 31
reaches EOL. We provide step-by-step upgrade instructions for <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/doc/template/fedora/upgrade/">upgrading
Fedora TemplateVMs</a>. For a complete list of TemplateVM versions
supported for your specific version of Qubes, see <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/doc/supported-versions/#templatevms">Supported TemplateVM
Versions</a>.</p>

<p>We also provide a fresh Fedora 32 TemplateVM package through the
official Qubes repositories, which you can install in dom0 by following
the standard <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/doc/templates/fedora/#installing">installation instructions</a>.</p>

<p>After upgrading your TemplateVMs, please remember to <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/doc/templates/#switching">switch all qubes
that were using the old template to use the new one</a>.</p>

<p>Please note that no user action is required regarding the OS version in
dom0. For details, please see our <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/doc/supported-versions/#note-on-dom0-and-eol">note on dom0 and EOL</a>.</p>


<hr>
<small>This is a companion discussion topic for the original entry at <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/news/2020/10/27/fedora-31-approaching-eol/">https://www.qubes-os.org/news/2020/10/27/fedora-31-approaching-eol/</a></small>

---

## XSAs 286, 331, 332, and 345 do not affect the security of Qubes OS

> 板块: News

<p>The Xen Project has published the following Xen Security Advisories:
XSA-286, XSA-331, XSA-332, and XSA-345. These XSAs do <strong>not</strong> affect the
security of Qubes OS, and no user action is necessary.</p>

<p><strong>Special note:</strong> Although XSA-345 is included in <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/news/2020/10/20/qsb-060/">QSB #060</a>, we do not
consider XSA-345 to affect the security of Qubes OS, since the default
configuration is safe, and we have already implemented appropriate
safeguards to prevent users from changing to a vulnerable configuration
by accident. Please see the Impact section of <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/news/2020/10/20/qsb-060/">QSB #060</a> for further
details.</p>

<p>These XSAs have been added to the <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/security/xsa/">XSA Tracker</a>:</p>

<p><a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/security/xsa/#286">https://www.qubes-os.org/security/xsa/#286</a><br>
<a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/security/xsa/#331">https://www.qubes-os.org/security/xsa/#331</a><br>
<a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/security/xsa/#332">https://www.qubes-os.org/security/xsa/#332</a><br>
<a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/security/xsa/#345">https://www.qubes-os.org/security/xsa/#345</a></p>


<hr>
<small>This is a companion discussion topic for the original entry at <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/news/2020/10/20/xsa-286-331-332-345-qubes-not-affected/">https://www.qubes-os.org/news/2020/10/20/xsa-286-331-332-345-qubes-not-affected/</a></small>

---

## QSB #060: Multiple Xen issues (XSA-345, XSA-346, XSA-347)

> 板块: News

<p>We have just published Qubes Security Bulletin (QSB) #060: Multiple Xen
issues (XSA-345, XSA-346, XSA-347). The text of this QSB is reproduced
below. This QSB and its accompanying signatures will always be available
in the Qubes Security Pack (qubes-secpack).</p>

<p><strong>Special note:</strong> Although XSA-345 is included in this QSB, <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/news/2020/10/20/xsa-286-331-332-345-qubes-not-affected/">we do not
consider XSA-345 to affect the security of Qubes OS</a>,
since the default configuration is safe, and we have already implemented
appropriate safeguards to prevent users from changing to a vulnerable
configuration by accident. Please see the Impact section in QSB #060
below for further details.</p>

<p>View QSB #060 in the qubes-secpack:</p>

<p><a href="https://github.com/QubesOS/qubes-secpack/blob/master/QSBs/qsb-060-2020.txt">https://github.com/QubesOS/qubes-secpack/blob/master/QSBs/qsb-060-2020.txt</a></p>

<p>Learn about the qubes-secpack, including how to obtain, verify, and read it:</p>

<p><a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/security/pack/">https://www.qubes-os.org/security/pack/</a></p>

<p>View all past QSBs:</p>

<p><a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/security/bulletins/">https://www.qubes-os.org/security/bulletins/</a></p>

<p>View the associated XSAs in the XSA Tracker:</p>

<p><a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/security/xsa/#345">https://www.qubes-os.org/security/xsa/#345</a><br>
<a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/security/xsa/#346">https://www.qubes-os.org/security/xsa/#346</a><br>
<a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/security/xsa/#347">https://www.qubes-os.org/security/xsa/#347</a></p>

<div class="language-plaintext highlighter-rouge"><div class="highlight"><pre class="highlight"><code>

             ---===[ Qubes Security Bulletin #60 ]===---

                             2020-10-20


           Multiple Xen issues (XSA-345, XSA-346, XSA-347)


Summary
========

On 2020-10-20, the Xen Security Team published the following Xen
Security Advisories (XSAs):

XSA-345 [1] "x86: Race condition in Xen mapping code":
| The Xen code handling the updating of the hypervisor's own pagetables
| tries to use 2MiB and 1GiB superpages as much as possible to maximize
| TLB efficiency.  Some of the operations for checking and coalescing
| superpages take non-negligible amount of time; to avoid potential lock
| contention, this code also tries to avoid holding locks for the entire
| operation.
| 
| Unfortunately, several potential race conditions were not considered;
| precisely-timed guest actions could potentially lead to the code
| writing to a page which has been freed (and thus potentially already
| reused).
| 
| A malicious guest can cause a host denial-of-service.  Data corruption
| or privilege escalation cannot be ruled out.


XSA-346 [2] "undue deferral of IOMMU TLB flushes":
| To efficiently change the physical to machine address mappings of a
| larger range of addresses for fully virtualized guests, Xen contains
| an optimization to coalesce per-page IOMMU TLB flushes into a single,
| wider flush after all adjustments have been made.  While this is fine
| to do for newly introduced page mappings, the possible removal of
| pages from such guests during this operation should not be "optimized"
| in the same way.  This is because the (typically) final reference of
| such pages is dropped before the coalesced flush, and hence the pages
| may have been put to a different use even though DMA initiated by
| their original owner might still be in progress.
| 
| A malicious guest might be able to cause data corruption and data
| leaks.  Host or guest Denial of Service (DoS), and privilege
| escalation, cannot be ruled out.


XSA-347 [3] "unsafe AMD IOMMU page table updates":
| AMD IOMMU page table entries are updated in a step by step manner,
| without regard to them being potentially in use by the IOMMU.
| Therefore it was possible that the IOMMU would read and then use a
| half-updated entry.  Furthermore, updates to Device Table entries
| lacked suitable ordering enforcement for certain steps involved in
| these updates.
| 
| In both case the specific outcome heavily depends on how exactly the
| compiler translated the affected pieces of code.
| 
| A malicious guest might be able to cause data corruption and data
| leaks.  Host or guest Denial of Service (DoS), and privilege
| escalation, cannot be ruled out.


Impact
=======

XSA-345: The default Qubes configuration is safe. Shadow mode for HVM
and PVH domains is disabled at build time, and domains that have PCI
devices run in HVM mode by default. Therefore, we do not consider this
XSA to affect the security of Qubes OS. However, we are including it in
this QSB anyway since it is technically possible for the user to
manually change a domain that has PCI devices from HVM to PV, which
would result in a configuration that is vulnerable to this issue. Having
anticipated the risk associated with such a manual change, we have
already implemented appropriate safeguards. In the Qubes GUI for
changing VM settings, the user would have to go to the "Advanced" tab in
order to change the setting from HVM to PV. Upon making the change, the
user would immediately be confronted with a warning in bold red text
that reads, "Using PV mode exposes more hypervisor attack surface!"
Therefore, it is nearly impossible users would switch to the vulnerable
configuration by accident.

XSA-346, XSA-347: A malicious domain with a PCI device (e.g., sys-net or
sys-usb in the default configuration) could try to exploit this
vulnerability in order to crash the host. Beyond DoS, it is unlikely
that this vulnerability could be exploited to compromise the system, but
we cannot completely rule out the possibility. Both of these issues
apply only to systems running on AMD processors.


Patching
=========

The specific packages that resolve the problems discussed in this
bulletin are as follows:

  For Qubes 4.0:
  - Xen packages, version 4.8.5-25
  For Qubes 4.1:
  - Xen packages, version 4.14.0-6
  
The packages are to be installed in dom0 via the Qube Manager or via
the qubes-dom0-update command as follows:

  For updates from the stable repository (not immediately available):
  $ sudo qubes-dom0-update

  For updates from the security-testing repository:
  $ sudo qubes-dom0-update --enablerepo=qubes-dom0-security-testing

A system restart will be required afterwards.

These packages will migrate from the security-testing repository to the
current (stable) repository over the next two weeks after being tested
by the community.

If you use Anti Evil Maid, you will need to reseal your secret
passphrase to new PCR values, as PCR18+19 will change due to the new
Xen binaries.


Credits
========

See the original Xen Security Advisories.


References
===========

[1] https://xenbits.xen.org/xsa/advisory-345.html
[1] https://xenbits.xen.org/xsa/advisory-346.html
[1] https://xenbits.xen.org/xsa/advisory-347.html

--
The Qubes Security Team
https://www.qubes-os.org/security/
</code></pre></div></div>


<hr>
<small>This is a companion discussion topic for the original entry at <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/news/2020/10/20/qsb-060/">https://www.qubes-os.org/news/2020/10/20/qsb-060/</a></small>

---

## Qubes OS contributed packages are now available

> 板块: News

<p>We are happy to announce the availability of Qubes OS contributed packages under the <a href="https://github.com/QubesOS-contrib/">QubesOS-contrib</a> GitHub Project. This is a place where our community can <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/doc/package-contributions/">contribute Qubes OS related packages, additions and various customizations</a>. Meanwhile, we provide the infrastructure and <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/doc/package-contributions/#review-procedure">review process</a> necessary to make them available easily and safely to users within standard Qubes installations.</p>

<p><a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/team/#fr%C3%A9d%C3%A9ric-pierret">Frédéric Pierret</a> built the infrastructure based on a similar setup for building official packages. This means that it features the same <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/news/2016/05/30/build-security/">Qubes build security</a> measures, including keeping the signing keys separate in a dedicated VM, downloading packages over Tor, publishing build logs in a non-spoofable way and more. Frédéric is also the maintainer of <a href="https://github.com/QubesOS-contrib/">QubesOS-contrib</a>.
The source code repositories of the packages and infrastructure-related parts are also hosted under <a href="https://github.com/QubesOS-contrib/">QubesOS-contrib</a>.</p>

<p>To contribute a package, follow the process described at <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/doc/package-contributions/">package contributions</a>. You will find a few helpful tips there, including a <a href="https://github.com/QubesOS-contrib/qubes-skeleton/">skeleton repository</a> with example RPM packaging and <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/doc/qubes-builder/">Qubes Builder</a> integration.
Since the project has been running for some time already, there are already some submitted packages available there. To name a few:</p>

<ul>
  <li><a href="https://github.com/QubesOS-contrib/qubes-tunnel">qubes-tunnel</a></li>
  <li><a href="https://github.com/QubesOS-contrib/qubes-qvm-screenshot-tool">qvm-screenshot-tool</a></li>
  <li><a href="https://github.com/QubesOS-contrib/qmenu">qmenu</a></li>
</ul>

<p>You can find the full list at <a href="https://github.com/QubesOS-contrib/">QubesOS-contrib</a>.</p>

<p>If you want to install one of these packages, first you need to enable the repository in your system (dom0 and/or templates). This can be done by installing the <code class="language-plaintext highlighter-rouge">qubes-repo-contrib</code> package. This package includes the repository definition and keys necessary to download, verify, and install <a href="https://github.com/QubesOS-contrib/">QubesOS-contrib</a> packages.</p>

<p>In dom0, use <code class="language-plaintext highlighter-rouge">qubes-dom0-update</code>:</p>

<div class="language-plaintext highlighter-rouge"><div class="highlight"><pre class="highlight"><code>sudo qubes-dom0-update qubes-repo-contrib
</code></pre></div></div>

<p>In a Fedora-based template, use <code class="language-plaintext highlighter-rouge">dnf</code>:</p>

<div class="language-plaintext highlighter-rouge"><div class="highlight"><pre class="highlight"><code>sudo dnf install qubes-repo-contrib
</code></pre></div></div>

<p>In a Debian-based template, use <code class="language-plaintext highlighter-rouge">apt</code>:</p>

<div class="language-plaintext highlighter-rouge"><div class="highlight"><pre class="highlight"><code>sudo apt update &amp;&amp; sudo apt install qubes-repo-contrib
</code></pre></div></div>


<hr>
<small>This is a companion discussion topic for the original entry at <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/news/2020/10/05/qubes-os-contributed-packages/">https://www.qubes-os.org/news/2020/10/05/qubes-os-contributed-packages/</a></small>

## Qubes wins MOSS Mission Partners award!

> 板块: News

<p>We’re proud to announce that the Qubes OS Project has won a
<a href="https://www.mozilla.org/en-US/moss/">Mozilla Open Source Support (MOSS)</a> award in the amount
of 128,000 USD. This generous one-year award will fund the work of
<a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/team/#frédéric-pierret">Frédéric Pierret</a> on making Qubes more robust and stable, as well as
several key parts of <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/team/#nina-eleanor-alter">Nina Alter</a>’s extensive work on making Qubes more
user-friendly.</p>
<hr>
<small>This is a companion discussion topic for the original entry at <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/news/2020/05/22/moss-mission-partners-award/">https://www.qubes-os.org/news/2020/05/22/moss-mission-partners-award/</a></small>

---

## Qubes Architecture Next Steps: The New Qrexec Policy System

> 板块: News

<p>This is the second article in the “What’s new in Qubes 4.1?” series. You
can find the previous one (about GUI Domains)
<a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/news/2020/03/18/gui-domain/">here</a>. While the
introduction of GUI domains is a big, singular feature, the changes to
qrexec are more complex and varied — but also very important.</p>

## Fedora 32 TemplateVMs available

> 板块: News

<p>New Fedora 32 TemplateVMs are now available for both Qubes 4.0 and 4.1.</p><p><strong>Important:</strong> If you wish to use the Qubes Update widget to update a
Fedora 32 template, you must first <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/doc/templates/#switching">switch</a> the
<code class="language-plaintext highlighter-rouge">default-mgmt-dvm</code> qube to a Fedora 32 template. (Alternatively, you
can create a separate management DisposableVM Template based on a
Fedora 32 template for the purpose of updating Fedora 32 templates.)
This does not affect updating internally using <code class="language-plaintext highlighter-rouge">dnf</code>.</p>
<hr>
<small>This is a companion discussion topic for the original entry at <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/news/2020/06/30/fedora-32-templates-available/">https://www.qubes-os.org/news/2020/06/30/fedora-32-templates-available/</a></small>

---

## XSAs 317, 319, 327, and 328 do not affect the security of Qubes OS

> 板块: News

<p>The Xen Project has published Xen Security Advisories 317, 319, 327, and
328 (XSA-317, XSA-319, XSA-327, and XSA-328, respectively). These
XSAs do <strong>not</strong> affect the security of Qubes OS, and no user action is
necessary.</p>
<hr>
<small>This is a companion discussion topic for the original entry at <a href="https://www.qubes-os.org/news/2020/07/07/xsa-317-319-327-328-qubes-not-affected/">https://www.qubes-os.org/news/2020/07/07/xsa-317-319-327-328-qubes-not-affected/</a></small>
